title: Cost Disease

content.statement: Work that resists automation gets more expensive as the rest of the economy speeds up.
content.explainer: When productivity soars in some sectors, wages rise everywhere to compete for the same workers, so labor-heavy work you can't speed up keeps costing more for the same output. It's why teaching, live service, and care get pricier even when nothing about them improves.
questions: Which of your costs keep rising even though that part of your work hasn't gotten any faster or better?
Are you pricing your labor-heavy services to keep pace with economy-wide wages, or quietly eating the gap?
Where are you fighting to automate work whose entire value depends on it staying human?
sop: List every part of your business where output per hour barely improves — hands-on service, teaching, coaching, care, or craft.
Estimate how fast wages are rising across the wider economy you compete with to hire and keep people.
Estimate the yearly productivity gain in that specific service line honestly — often near zero.
Subtract sector productivity growth from economy wage growth to project each line's built-in cost creep (see calculation, page 6).
Reprice those services on a schedule or redesign delivery, so the rising cost doesn't silently erase your margin.

definitions.def: How much output a worker produces per hour. In some work — teaching, live music, care — it is hard to raise without losing the quality that makes it valuable.
The tendency for wages and costs in low-productivity work to climb because those sectors must compete for workers with high-productivity ones, even when they gain no efficiency themselves.

content.equation: Cost Growth = Economy Wage Growth − Sector Productivity Growth
content.equation_example: Economy-wide wages rise 4% a year; your service's productivity rises 1%. 4% − 1% = 3% — your costs climb about 3% annually even with zero change in quality. A bigger wage-minus-productivity gap means faster cost creep; a smaller gap means your costs stay flatter.
content.closing_statement: The work that machines can't rush — care, teaching, craft — is exactly what's worth paying to keep human.
content.question: Which of your costs keep rising even though that part of your work hasn't gotten any faster or better?

caption:
Principle Nº 336: Cost Disease

Work that resists automation gets more expensive as the rest of the economy speeds up.

Ask yourself: Which of your costs keep rising even though that part of your work hasn't gotten any faster or better?
